passage:
Text 1
Solar panels installed on rooftops have become increasingly common in residential neighborhoods. A homeowner advocacy group reports that households with rooftop solar panels reduce their annual electricity bills by an average of 40 to 60 percent compared to households without solar panels. The group notes that the savings are most significant in regions with high average sunlight hours per day.

Text 2
A utility company study examined electricity consumption patterns across three regions: the Southwest (average 7.5 sunlight hours/day), the Midwest (average 4.8 sunlight hours/day), and the Northeast (average 4.1 sunlight hours/day). In the Southwest, solar panel households saved an average of $1,200 per year. In the Midwest, solar panel households saved an average of $650 per year. In the Northeast, solar panel households saved an average of $480 per year.

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from homeowner advocacy and utility company reports

stem: Based on both texts, which claim about solar panel savings is best supported?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Savings from solar panels are greatest in regions with more daily sunlight.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Solar panels reduce electricity bills by exactly 40 percent in all regions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Midwest households save more than Southwest households annually.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Northeast households receive no financial benefit from solar panels.

explanation: Text 1 states that savings are "most significant in regions with high average sunlight hours per day." Text 2 confirms this directly: the Southwest, with the highest sunlight average (7.5 hours/day), yields the greatest savings ($1,200/year), followed by the Midwest ($650/year) and then the Northeast ($480/year) with the fewest sunlight hours. Together, both texts consistently support the claim that more sunlight leads to greater savings.

distractorRationale.B: Text 1 states savings range from 40 to 60 percent on average — not exactly 40 percent in all regions. The data in Text 2 also shows varying savings amounts by region, contradicting a flat 40 percent claim.
distractorRationale.C: Text 2 explicitly shows Midwest households save $650/year while Southwest households save $1,200/year, making this a direct misreading of the data — the Midwest saves less, not more.
distractorRationale.D: Text 2 shows Northeast households save an average of $480 per year, which is a meaningful financial benefit. This distractor contradicts the explicit data in Text 2.
